title: SIR Epidemic Model
text:
This project implements an agent-based SIR epidemic model using object-oriented programming (OOP) principles.
It simulates how a disease spreads through a population over time.



The population is divided into three states:

Susceptible (S) – healthy individuals who can become infected

Infected (I) – individuals who can spread the disease

Recovered (R) – individuals who are no longer infectious





This project is structured using OOP to separate concerns and improve reusability:

Population class
Handles initialization of agents and their states
SIRSimulator class
Encapsulates the simulation logic and time evolution
VisualizerSIR class
Responsible for plotting and displaying results

This modular structure allows the model to be easily extended (e.g., to SEIR).





import sys
from pathlib import Path

PROJECT_ROOT = Path().resolve().parents[1]
sys.path.append(str(PROJECT_ROOT))

from models.sir.sir_simulator import SIRSimulator
from models.sir.visualizer_sir import VisualizerSIR

# Initialize simulation
sim = SIRSimulator(
    N=500,
    I0=0.01,
    beta=0.2,
    mu=0.1,
    t_max=200
)

# Run simulation
history = sim.run()

# Visualize results
viz = VisualizerSIR(history, title="SIR Model Infection Dynamics")
viz.plot()











You can explore the model interactively:


🔗 Open in full screen

title: Projects
text:
This page showcases my work in data science, simulation modeling, and machine learning. Each project includes code, methodology, and key insights.



Agent-based simulation of disease spread using object-oriented programming principles.

Models infection, recovery, and population dynamics

Modular design (Simulator + Visualizer architecture)

Demonstrates epidemic peak behavior and stabilization
View Project





An extension of the SIR model that introduces an Exposed (latent) compartment for more realistic epidemic modeling.

Includes incubation period before infection

Improved realism compared to SIR model

Built with reusable and extensible OOP structure
View Project





Exploratory data analysis of energy-related datasets, focusing on trends, comparisons, and insights into consumption and production patterns.

View Project





Machine learning project focused on predicting ocean conditions using environmental data.

Applies ML models to oceanographic variables

Predicts significant wave height

Evaluates model performance and accuracy
View Project

title: About Me
text:
My name is Mykhaylo Orlov. I am a graduating Bachelor’s student at the Central European University in Vienna.
I major in Data Science and Society, with a minor in Environmental Sciences.

title: About Me
text:
Academic Background
My studies cover a broad range of scientific disciplines, including computer science, statistics, physics, and computational social sciences. This interdisciplinary foundation shapes how I approach data-driven problems.

title: About Me
text:
Areas of Interest

Climate change and data-driven environmental analysis

Computational social science and modeling of social systems

Applied mathematics in natural and social sciences

Computer science and development of scientific tools and applications

title: About Me
text:
About My Work
I am particularly interested in using data science and modeling to better understand complex real-world systems, especially in environmental and social contexts. If you are more interested in my working experience, please refer to the Resume page.

title: Home
text:
Welcome to my portfolio. Here you will find a selection of my data science projects developed during my studies.
For more detailed descriptions, please visit the Projects page.





These projects explore how infectious diseases spread using classical epidemiological models (SIR and SEIR), applied under different scenarios and assumptions.




An exploratory data analysis of global fossil fuel emissions, focusing on trends, patterns, and environmental impact over time.




A machine learning project aimed at predicting significant wave height using environmental and oceanographic data.





This site showcases my work in data science, including modeling, data analysis, and machine learning projects. Each project includes code, methodology, and insights gained from the analysis.

title: Energy Data Analysis
text:
This project analyzes energy consumption data using an object-oriented pipeline.




DataLoader → loads dataset

DataProcessor → cleans and transforms data

Visualizer → creates plots

AnalysisApp → orchestrates workflow

sys.path.append(str(PROJECT_ROOT))

data_path = PROJECT_ROOT / "models" / "data_analysis" / "data" / "file.csv"
app = AnalysisApp(str(data_path))

df = app.run()

title: Machine Learning Pipeline
text:
This project implements a modular machine learning pipeline using object-oriented design.
It handles data loading, preprocessing, model training, evaluation, and result export in a single workflow.



The pipeline follows these main steps:

Data Loading – reads raw data from disk

Data Cleaning – handles preprocessing and feature preparation

Model Training – trains multiple models for comparison

Evaluation – selects the best model based on RMSE

Output Generation – saves predictions and visualizations





The pipeline is encapsulated in the MLPipeline class:

load_and_prepare()
Loads dataset and splits it into training and validation sets
train_and_evaluate()
Trains all models and stores their RMSE scores
select_best_model()
Chooses the best-performing model
run()
Executes the full workflow end-to-end

This structure makes the pipeline reusable, testable, and easy to extend.





import sys
from pathlib import Path

PROJECT_ROOT = Path().resolve().parents[1]
sys.path.append(str(PROJECT_ROOT))

from models.ml_project.src.pipeline import MLPipeline

# Initialize and run pipeline
pipeline = MLPipeline()
results = pipeline.run()

print("Best model:", results["best_model_name"])
print("RMSE:", results["rmse"])

Best model: random_forest
RMSE: 0.08658417539539033






After execution, the pipeline generates:

outputs/predictions.csv – model predictions vs actual values

outputs/figures/feature_importance.png – feature importance plot (if available)





The pipeline can be easily extended by:

Adding new models in get_models()

Improving preprocessing in clean_data()

Adding additional evaluation metrics

Integrating hyperparameter tuning

title: SEIR Epidemic Model
text:
This project extends the SIR model by adding an Exposed (E) state and uses OOP principles to simulate the epidemic dynamics.



PopulationSEIR class: Initializes agents (Susceptible, Exposed, Infected, Recovered)

SEIRSimulator class: Runs the simulation over time

Visualizer class: Handles plotting results





import sys
from pathlib import Path

PROJECT_ROOT = Path().resolve().parents[1]
sys.path.append(str(PROJECT_ROOT))

from models.seir.seir_simulator import SEIRSimulator
from models.seir.visualizer_seir import VisualizerSEIR

# initialize SEIR simulation
sim = SEIRSimulator(N=500, I0=0.01, beta=0.3, sigma=0.2, mu=0.1, t_max=200)
history = sim.run()

# visualize the results
viz = VisualizerSEIR(history, title="SEIR Model Infection Dynamics")
viz.plot()











You can explore the model interactively:


Open in full screen
